M-F1.title: The First Anomaly
M-F1.text: Under the Limehouse Basin the water carries a note — B-flat, sustained, source unknown. The boat painters hear it in winter when the engines stop. They don't discuss it. They hum along without noticing.

M-F2.title: The Session Tape
M-F2.text: In 1967 a recording engineer set up equipment in a Narrow Street basement. The session tapes contain voices he didn't record — a woman singing in a dialect forty years out of date. He labelled the tape "background" and never listened again. The tape is in the pub's walls now.

M-F5.title: Hawksmoor's Frequency
M-F5.text: Hawksmoor's churches were built to specific acoustic specifications — St Anne's resonates at B-flat, the same note as the canal, the same note the barista hums. The question nobody asks: does the church amplify the city's sound, or does the city tune itself to the church?

P-F1.title: The Water's Exposure
P-F1.text: At certain angles the basin surface holds light in patterns that don't match the sky — reflections of masts, rigging, lanterns, none of which are there. You look away, look back. Still there, older than anything on this canal.

P-F2.title: The Impossible Angles
P-F2.text: Inside St Anne's the shadows fall wrong. They lean toward the altar at angles the windows cannot produce. Hawksmoor built the geometry to bend light — or the light here bends itself, following instructions older than the architecture.

P-F3.title: The Returning Gaze
P-F3.text: Behind the bar, above the gin shelf, a framed photograph — the building as it was, timber frame, no glass in the windows. A figure in the doorway staring directly at the lens. The publican says it's from 1890. The building is older than photography.

W-F1.title: The Cold Seam
W-F1.text: Three steps past the lock gate your ankles go cold — not the air, the ground. Dog walkers cross to the other side without deciding to. Something is held here, not buried, not hidden. Just cold.

W-F2.title: The Earth's Floor Plan
W-F2.text: The grass on the empty lot grows in straight lines — walls, corridors, a threshold you can feel underfoot where the door was. The building was demolished fifty years ago but the earth still keeps the floor plan. It outlines rooms for tenants who won't return.

W-F3.title: The Drowned Path
W-F3.text: Walking the basin edge your legs feel heavy, resisted, as if wading. The canal was cut through marshland. Your body remembers the water the engineers removed. The ankles know what was here before the path.

B-F1.title: The Table's Choreography
B-F1.text: The corner table seats two strangers every morning who never know each other. They always end up confessing — about leaving somewhere, arriving somewhere else. The same words, different mouths. Every morning.

B-F2.title: The Market's Sentence
B-F2.text: The fish seller says "not long now" and three stalls down the flower woman finishes: "before it all changes." They've never spoken. They don't know they're connected.

B-F3.title: The Regulars' Impression
B-F3.text: The same four seats worn smooth at the bar, decades of the same bodies in the same positions. The wood has shaped itself to them — or they've shaped themselves to the wood. The publican says regulars don't choose their seats; the seats choose them back.

S-F1.title: The Fading Doors
S-F1.text: The oldest flash art on the parlour wall has faded to outlines — doorways, arches, a narrow passage. The tattooist says they're stock designs, but the doors they depict are real: three addresses in Limehouse, all demolished. The drawings outlasted the buildings.

S-F2.title: The Orphaned Coin
S-F2.text: A coin wedged in the platform edge, green with age, its denomination from a currency discontinued before the DLR existed. Someone asks the attendant about it yearly; no one removes it. Objects outlast the systems that gave them meaning.

S-F3.title: The Rewritten Stone
S-F3.text: A gravestone near the east wall has been recarved three times. Each inscription older than the last — the newest name from 1780, the deepest from centuries before. The stone keeps choosing whose name to hold.

M-F3.title: The Retracted Paper
M-F3.text: Limestone absorbs vibration. The clay beneath London is forty metres deep in places. A geoacoustics paper from 1983 proposed that certain geological formations could store and replay sound energy. The paper was retracted. The author moved to Limehouse.

P-F1.title: The Contaminated Brushstroke
P-F1.text: The Painted Hall ceiling contains 40,000 square feet of painted surface. A 2015 restoration documented every inch. In the north-east corner, behind Neptune's trident, they found a brushstroke in a pigment not manufactured until 1923. The restoration report lists it as contamination. Nobody contaminated it.

P-F3.title: The Sharper Reflection
P-F3.text: A daguerreotype from 1842 of the Greenwich waterfront shows, in the reflection on the Thames, a building that wasn't on the north bank until 1856. Fourteen years early. The reflection is sharper than the subject — as if the river's version was more real than the photographer's.

P-F7.title: The Thames as Mirror
P-F7.text: The Thames is London's mirror. Every edition of the city has looked at its reflection in the river and seen itself. But a mirror doesn't just show what's there. A mirror reverses. What if the Thames shows London not as it is, but as its negative — the version that exists on the other side of the reflection? And what if that version is more complete?

W-F2.title: The Reluctant Clay
W-F2.text: In 1902 when the Greenwich tunnel was dug, workers reported a reluctance — not their own — at a specific depth. The clay resisted. Not physically. Emotionally. Three workers quit on the same day. They described, independently, a feeling of being watched by something that was not above them.

W-F4.title: The Park's Expectation
W-F4.text: Greenwich Park at dawn, before the joggers and the dog walkers, holds a feeling that doesn't belong to the present. It's older. Not sad. Not happy. Anticipatory. As if the park is waiting for someone it expected centuries ago. The feeling dissipates by 7 AM. The park pretends it was never there.

BA-F2.title: The Web
BA-F2.text: In 1888, a vicar in Bermondsey compiled a list of every person who had lived on his street for the previous century. Every family had a link to at least one other. The vicar called it the web. He stopped compiling when the web began to include people who hadn't been born yet.

BA-F5.title: The City as Bartender
BA-F5.text: Imagine a city that knows its people the way a bartender knows regulars. Every street a bartender. Every corner a regular. The city takes your order before you know what you want. It seats you next to the person you need to meet. And if someone is removed from the bar, everyone feels the missing stool.

BA-F6.title: The Broken Web
BA-F6.text: A page torn from a journal, found pressed inside a hymnal at St Alfege. The handwriting is controlled but the content is not: I erased them because the alternative was worse. A single node carrying the weight of every connection in London. I broke the web to save it. I broke the web. I broke it.

S-F2.title: The Seven Stones
S-F2.text: Seven stones in London have never moved. Not since they were placed. The London Stone on Cannon Street is the famous one. Six others are unmarked, embedded in walls and foundations across the city. Each predates the building it supports. Each weighs more than it should. The clockmaker's shop is built on one.

P-F2.title: The Absent Buildings
P-F2.text: The London Survey of 1746 recorded every building on Bermondsey Street. Three buildings in the survey do not appear on any subsequent map. They were not demolished — no demolition record exists. They are simply absent from every document after 1746. The addresses still receive mail.

P-F5.title: The City's Sentence
P-F5.text: From the rooftop of the railway arch, the streets of Bermondsey form letters. Not in any alphabet currently in use. The letters are legible only from above and only at twilight when shadows extend the buildings' edges. Someone designed this. Not the urban planners. The city arranged itself into a sentence nobody has read.

W-F3.title: The Accumulated Feeling
W-F3.text: Every building accumulates the emotions of its inhabitants. Hospitals feel different from schools. Prisons feel different from libraries. This isn't metaphor — studies in environmental psychology measure it. But nobody has measured a city. Nobody has asked what two thousand years of accumulated feeling produces. Perhaps because the answer moves.

W-F7.title: London's Weight
W-F7.text: London has dreamed every night for two thousand years. Dreams process what the waking mind cannot. If London were to wake — if the dream ended — where would the unprocessed go? The sadness of every goodbye at every train station. The joy of every first kiss on every bridge. The weight of it. Would London survive its own waking?

W-F6.title: The Warning on the Railing
W-F6.text: A message scratched into the Thames Path railing near Rotherhithe, so faint you'd miss it unless you were looking. Or unless you were feeling. It reads: 'It noticed me in 2017. I noticed it back. That was my mistake. The noticing doesn't stop. Walk away if you can.'

BA-F3.title: Three Degrees
BA-F3.text: Network theory says six degrees separate any two people on earth. In London the number is three. In East London it's two. The connections aren't social media friendships. They're spatial — sharing a bus, breathing the same air, walking the same pavement within minutes of each other. The city is a contact network. Everyone touches everyone.

BA-F7.title: The Erased Name
BA-F7.text: The erased name is not gone. It's distributed. Split across every person who ever knew them, like a word broken into letters scattered through a book. The letters are still there. The word can be reassembled. But should it be? A name that powerful, a connection that total — is that a person, or is that something a person should never be?

S-F1.title: The Key
S-F1.text: The key has no manufacturer's mark from any known foundry. The alloy contains traces of a mineral found only in the chalk deposits beneath the Thames Basin. The mineral has no industrial application. It has been in the ground since before the ice retreated. The key was made from the ground itself.

S-F5.title: The People Who Feel the Ground
S-F5.text: Some people feel the ground more than others. Not seismologists — people with hands. Bricklayers who know which wall will stand. Gardeners who feel the soil's mood. Shopkeepers who've stood on the same floor for thirty years and can tell you when the building sighs. These people don't know what they know. But the ground knows them.

S-F6.title: Still Sleeping
S-F6.text: A survey marker embedded in the warehouse floor. Official-looking but not from any known survey agency. Dated 2020. The coordinates are correct. The depth reading is not — it shows a measurement 400 metres below ground level. Standard surveys reach 30. Next to the marker, scratched in the concrete: 'Still sleeping. Check annually.'
